mission.title: Install and Configure Falcosidekick on Kubernetes
mission.description: Falcosidekick is a daemon that connects Falco to your ecosystem, forwarding Falco events to various outputs such as Slack, Teams, Elasticsearch, Loki, and many more. Installing Falcosidekick enhances your security monitoring by integrating Falco alerts with your existing tools and workflows.

steps.title: Add the Falco Security Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Falco Security Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Falcosidekick using Helm
steps.description:
Install Falcosidekick in your Kubernetes cluster. This creates a dedicated namespace for Falcosidekick:
```bash
helm install falcosidekick falcosecurity/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick --create-namespace --version 0.13.0
```

Note: Falcosidekick requires Falco to be running in your cluster to receive events. You can install Falco with Falcosidekick enabled together:
```bash
helm install falco falcosecurity/falco --namespace falco --create-namespace --set falcosidekick.enabled=true
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Falcosidekick pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falcosidekick
```

You should see one or more pods with STATUS 'Running'.

steps.title: Verify Falcosidekick is receiving events
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Falcosidekick web UI and verify events are flowing:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick 2801:2801
```

Then check the health endpoint:
```bash
curl http://localhost:2801/healthz
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation shows the Falcosidekick pod running in the 'falcosidekick' namespace. The service is accessible on port 2801 and forwards Falco events to configured outputs.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Falcosidekick Helm release:
```bash
helm uninstall falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'falcosidekick' namespace to clean up all remaining resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace falcosidekick
```

upgrade.title: Update Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to get the latest chart versions:
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Falcosidekick
upgrade.description:
Upgrade to a newer chart version:
```bash
helm upgrade falcosidekick falcosecurity/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Falcosidekick pod is running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falcosidekick
```

troubleshooting.title: Pod in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falcosidekick pod is in CrashLoopBackOff, check the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```
Common causes include misconfigured output destinations or connectivity issues.

troubleshooting.title: No events received
troubleshooting.description:
If Falcosidekick is running but not receiving events, verify that Falco is installed and configured to send events to Falcosidekick. Check Falco's configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falco
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falco --namespace falco | tail -20
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If port forwarding fails, verify the service exists:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace falcosidekick
```

prerequisites.description: Requires a Kubernetes cluster with Helm and kubectl installed. Falco should be running in the cluster to generate security events that Falcosidekick forwards to configured outputs.
